title: I believe the pilots are human
display_title: I believe the pilots are human
wikitext:
[]: I believe the pilots are human             Quote Post by pwhodges » Sat Mar 06, 2021 12:55 pm You know, I'm actually not happy with the idea that Shinji and the other pilots are not (or no longer) human. In my mind that would split them too far from the humanity whose mental and societal problems they struggle with, and whose resolution is the true end-point of this all. The lore around the other possibility is fascinating, and at times awesome, but in the end it is, for me, too far devolved from the heart of Evangelion - however convincing it may be in itself.

[]: Re: I believe the pilots are human             Quote Post by Blockio » Sat Mar 06, 2021 1:09 pm I disagree on the grounds that the concept of humanity has always been a rather soft one in Eva. I do believe that the pilots are not/no longer biologically Lilin , but that doesn't mean that they aren't human , for all that entails.

[]: Re: I believe the pilots are human             Quote Post by Joseki » Sat Mar 06, 2021 1:09 pm I completely agree. Personally anything other then them being "human" (or mostly human) would drastically decrease my enjoyment of the movies.

[]: Re: I believe the pilots are human             Quote Post by Archer » Sat Mar 06, 2021 1:19 pm I’d be disappointed if they were revealed to have been the reincarnations of gods this whole time, because then it turns from a story about things that happen to normal people into a stupid chosen-one story where they had a hidden destiny from the very beginning. However, at this point I think it’s pretty safe to assume that they’re no longer fully human just by virtue of piloting the Evas (...or by exceeding plug depth, who knows).

[]: Re: I believe the pilots are human             Quote Post by FXArmaros98 » Sat Mar 06, 2021 1:20 pm Blockio wrote: I do believe that the pilots are not/no longer biologically Lilin , but that doesn't mean that they aren't human , for all that entails.

[]: Re: I believe the pilots are human             Quote Post by ChaddyManPrime » Sat Mar 06, 2021 1:28 pm I believe that the humans of Eva were never human, like lilins were biologically not what we are, they are literally an angel just called humanity. But Lilin+ doesn't really matter a whole lot, like what was their genetic similarity to adamites already? Like 99.99999999999999999999%?

[]: Re: I believe the pilots are human             Quote Post by silvermoonlight » Sat Mar 06, 2021 1:40 pm I do think Asuka either broke the glass in 3.33 as she has inhuman strength as part of Bardiel is still inside her or her left arm is a robotic along with her eye as 02 is missing an arm (I'm hopeful that the final movie will reveal this) SPOILER: Show

[]: Re: I believe the pilots are human             Quote Post by EscapismIsBad » Sat Mar 06, 2021 2:09 pm Archer wrote: I’d be disappointed if they were revealed to have been the reincarnations of gods this whole time, because then it turns from a story about things that happen to normal people into a stupid chosen-one story where they had a hidden destiny from the very beginning.

[]: Re: I believe the pilots are human             Quote Post by BernardoCairo » Sat Mar 06, 2021 2:14 pm Archer wrote: I’d be disappointed if they were revealed to have been the reincarnations of gods this whole time, because then it turns from a story about things that happen to normal people into a stupid chosen-one story where they had a hidden destiny from the very beginning.

[]: Re: I believe the pilots are human             Quote Post by FXArmaros98 » Sat Mar 06, 2021 2:28 pm Bardiel is still inside her or her left arm is a robotic along with her eye as 02 is missing an arm (I'm hopeful that the final movie will reveal this)

[]: Re: I believe the pilots are human             Quote Post by baldur » Sat Mar 06, 2021 3:26 pm I don't really feel strongly one way or another, but I personally don't have a problem if they're not. In fact, I think it might add something very interesting to the characters, both directly and thematically. I still think of characters like Rei and Kaworu as being "human" even if they're not technically Lilin. Evangelion has always had a loose definition of what it means to be "human", so it's not like a revelation like this would be coming out of nowhere.

[]: Re: I believe the pilots are human             Quote Post by Blockio » Sat Mar 06, 2021 3:29 pm baldur wrote: I still think of characters like Rei and Kaworu as being "human" even if they're not technically Lilin. Evangelion has always had a loose definition of what it means to be "human", so it's not like a revelation like this would be coming out of nowhere.

[]: Re: I believe the pilots are human             Quote Post by baldur » Sat Mar 06, 2021 3:49 pm EscapismIsBad wrote: The whole destiny stuff i think it's far fetched too, it removes some of the impact of human consequences.

[]: Re: I believe the pilots are human             Quote Post by FXArmaros98 » Sat Mar 06, 2021 3:50 pm Asuka ominously talking about "The Linin" at the end of Q as if she's not a part of that group anymore, that does paint a certain picture.

[]: Re: I believe the pilots are human             Quote Post by Electreel » Sat Mar 06, 2021 4:04 pm 3.0 goes out of its way to show that the pilots are no longer Lilin (if they once were), by making them ageless, by giving them superhuman strength and the ability to survive the L Barrier without a hazmat suit, and by having them refer to other humans as 'Lilin'. There's no question about that. But whether they lost their humanity by going too far down an entry plug or they are reincarnations of the Adams & Lilith, I don't see how this affects the way we see them as human beings. I mean, let's not forget that Rei was basically the reincarnation of a divine being in a human body in NGE (this is still not confirmed to be the case in NTE, but we can infer that it is). Like all the main characters she has a definite personality, has complex thoughts and feelings, and establishes relationships with other people, and it is through these interactions that her character evolves throughout the series. The fact that she's not human in the ordinary sense of the word shouldn't affect the way we feel about her. Only at the end does she become something more than just a human being and ends up being detached from the rest of the characters, but there's no indication this is going to happen to the pilots in 3.0+1.0. Or let's consider another of Anno's works, Nadia: The Mystery of Blue Water. (hidden because of spoilers, duh). SPOILER: Show

[]: Re: I believe the pilots are human             Quote Post by Zusuchan » Sat Mar 06, 2021 4:20 pm Very well said, Electreel. I think the Children not being "Lilin" is pretty much a fact already. As for the artistic merits of having them be "non-human", I have to agree-the idea that the same characters many people obviously feel a close connection with somehow lose their normalcy the moment they become distinctly less human is strange to me. Even beyond the fact that Rei and Kaworu are already widely beloved and relatable characters who shine a light on parts of humanity despite being "Angels" is proof enough of the fact these things don't really matter. Of course there's the argument that in NGE, the Lilin are Angels and vice versa, but even then neither Rei nor Kaworu are normal in the literal sense of the word and are pretty consistently portrayed as otherworldly and mysterious in NGE and EoE as well. Even beyond that, I don't think Chewbacca's or R2-D2's characters (to take examples from one of the most popular franchises worldwide) exactly are somehow lesser and harder to empathize with because they're not human. The entirety of mankind's creative history is full of characters who can't be considered human, but are still relatable, because of how they exhibit several characteristics pertaining to real humans in the real world. And I think this here is pretty much the same thing-Anno probably has a good reason for making the Children not Lilin, but the power of the story shouldn't automatically lessen because of that. Regardless of whether or not Shinji is the reincarnation of a god, he goes through the exact same issues, issues that are profoundly human and that many can relate to, have gone through, are going through etc. I consider only characters who are distinctly non-human in their emotional worldview (such as Lovecraft's cosmic gods, for example) impossible to relate to, though of course that might very well be the point (it certainly was for Lovecraft). But the point is I don't really see how Shinji being not human is something that detracts from his story automatically.

[]: Re: I believe the pilots are human             Quote Post by ChaddyManPrime » Sat Mar 06, 2021 4:22 pm Aww man I'd love it if Shinji and Asuka were revealed as deities, man that'd work so perfectly.

[]: Re: I believe the pilots are human             Quote Post by BernardoCairo » Sat Mar 06, 2021 4:24 pm I don't have any problem with the characters becoming something that is not "lilin" because of their constant exposure to Evangelions, or something like that. I don't really know where the writers are going with this. However, the story isn't over yet. So, I have to wait for 3.0 + 1.0 to come out and then draw my own conclusions. What don't appetize me is the idea that our protagonists are reincarnations of gods. Personally, I think that's boring. I mean, how many stories like that have we already got? Besides, I'm also not a fan of ​​turning every event in the series into works of "destiny". It makes everything seems more artificial, when it doesn't need to be. "Oh, but Ayanami and Kaworu were always devine creatures and you are not complaining about it." Well, that's because that was their entire gimmick since the beginning. It is something that contributes to their “drama”, in addition to highlighting them from other characters. In contrast, I've always loved the more "grounded" nature of Shinji, Asuka and Misato. For me, it is fun to see these unfortunate humans dealing with things that they are not able to comprehend (nor control) while also suffering from their own internal struggles and so on. These are characters that feel like real people (specially during episodes such as 15). Turning some of them into gods would not add anything valuable, in my opinion. Anyway, that's all a matter of taste, I guess.

[]: Re: I believe the pilots are human             Quote Post by FXArmaros98 » Sat Mar 06, 2021 4:24 pm 3.0 goes out of its way to show that the pilots are no longer Lilin (if they once were), by making them ageless, by giving them superhuman strength and the ability to survive the L Barrier without a hazmat suit, and by having them refer to other humans as 'Lilin'.

The fact that she's not human in the ordinary sense of the word shouldn't affect the way we feel about her. Only at the end does she become something more than just a human being and ends up being detached from the rest of the characters, but there's no indication this is going to happen to the pilots in 3.0+1.0.

Article: I believe the pilots are human
ilin'. There's no question about that. But whether they lost their humanity by going too far down an entry plug or they are reincarnations of the Adams & Lilith, I don't see how this affects the way we see them as human beings. I mean, let's not forget that Rei was basically the reincarnation of a divine being in a human body in NGE (this is still not confirmed to be the case in NTE, but we can infer that it is). Like all the main characters she has a definite personality, has complex thoughts and feelings, and establishes relationships with other people, and it is through these interactions that her character evolves throughout the series. The fact that she's not human in the ordinary sense of the word shouldn't affect the way we feel about her. Only at the end does she become something more than just a human being and ends up being detached from the rest of the characters, but there's no indication this is going to happen to the pilots in 3.0+1.0. Or let's consider another of Anno's works, Nadia: The Mystery of Blue Water. (hidden because of spoilers, duh). SPOILER: Show

Even beyond that, I don't think Chewbacca's or R2-D2's characters (to take examples from one of the most popular franchises worldwide) exactly are somehow lesser and harder to empathize with because they're not human.
